topics.headline: California's 2026-27 budget just handed charter schools a $5B "spend it how you want" block grant — the intervention window is open NOW
topics.why_matters: Every Tier A partner is a California charter. This budget cycle just increased their per-pupil resources materially and — crucially — created a large discretionary pool. Per-pupil funding hits $27,418 per student across all funding sources and $20,427 per student under the Proposition 98 General Fund, with a 4.31% COLA and a massive one-time Student Support and Professional Development Block Grant of $5 billion. The LAO's earlier framing makes the intervention hook explicit: districts receive funding based on average daily attendance — roughly $512 per student — and the grant would not have specific spending requirements, though trailer legislation suggests potential uses including teacher professional development and career pathways, with funds spendable any time before June 30, 2030. Meanwhile $1 billion in ongoing Prop 98 funds will expand community schools to sites with large concentrations of English learners, foster youth, and low-income students — exactly A+'s LTEL profile.
topics.angle: "The state just gave charter schools a $5B flexible block grant with a 2030 deadline. Here's the three-question framework A+ uses with partners to convert those dollars into reclassification outcomes."
topics.roman_take: Flexibility without a plan is how districts leave money on the table — the conviction post is about the difference between "we got the grant" and "we moved the metric," and why the 4-year runway is a trap without an intervention design decision made in Q1.
topics.danielle_take: Practical walkthrough of what a special programs coordinator should be putting into their August board packet — LCAP language, evidence-tier documentation, and the mechanics of braiding the new block grant with existing Title III Immigrant carryover so nothing expires. ---

topics.headline: iLEAD Schools launches iLEAD Flex in Lancaster — first Tier A partner to move on the lifted NCB moratorium
topics.category: C (Tier A partner news; also touches A — charter policy)
topics.why_matters: iLEAD is one of A+'s largest Tier A partners (multiple locations across LA + Ventura). With California's moratorium on new non-classroom-based charter schools lifted this year, iLEAD is expanding into new communities, announcing a new charter in Lancaster beginning in 2026. iLEAD Flex opens in August 2026, offering families a choice between full classroom-based instruction, Independent Study, or hybrid options on campus each week. This is the first visible signal that the post-moratorium growth wave is real — and Lancaster is precisely a community where intervention demand is acute: iLEAD Lancaster Charter District currently has 17% of students proficient in reading and 10% in math, well below the California averages of 43% and 35%. New site, low baseline outcomes, 83% economically disadvantaged, hybrid model = textbook intervention services opportunity, on top of an existing partner relationship.
topics.angle: Congratulate iLEAD on the Flex launch and frame the broader question — what does responsible charter growth in the post-moratorium era look like when authorizer scrutiny is only going to tighten?
topics.roman_take: The moratorium didn't lift so networks could sprint blind — it lifted so mission-driven networks could grow with proof-of-outcomes infrastructure. The next round of authorizer fights will be won by charters that can show, not tell.
topics.danielle_take: When you launch a new campus, your intervention plan can't be Year 2 work — it has to be in the launch playbook. Here's what "Tier 2 from day one" actually looks like operationally.

topics.headline: Stanford/NSSA RCT finds "access to AI tutors is not the same as using them" — human support is the missing ingredient
topics.why_matters: Two RCTs found that nearly half of students in the AI-only control group never used the platform, those who did averaged only 2-5 minutes per week, and even with a human tutor focused on engagement, weekly platform usage only rose 1 to 4 minutes and reading achievement did not improve — leading researchers to conclude that access alone is insufficient and human support matters for meaningful engagement with AI-based learning tools. This is the single strongest piece of external evidence in 2026 that A+'s model (real humans, high-dosage, embedded) is the model that actually moves students. It's also a direct rebuttal to every "AI tutor will replace intervention" pitch charter directors have been fielding for 18 months. The study also aligns with the experience of Khanmigo founder Sal Khan, who recently acknowledged that students didn't engage much with the AI tutor he initially hoped would revolutionize education.
topics.angle: Lead with the RCT finding, connect to A+'s consistent-tutor / small-group model, close with the "human relationship is the intervention" thesis. Include one A+ case study data point.
topics.roman_take: Conviction post — "The AI tutor gold rush just hit its first RCT wall. We told you: relationships are the intervention, not the interface." Name Khanmigo directly.
topics.danielle_take: Implementation post — What this study means for a special programs coordinator writing next year's MTSS plan: don't budget Title I dollars against a login rate, budget them against attended sessions with a named tutor. ---
